walkthrough.title: Gather Your Materials
walkthrough.text: Let's set up Gin Rummy. All you need is a standard 52-card deck and something to keep score with, like pen and paper. No special boards or tokens required. This is a classic two-player card game.

walkthrough.title: Know the Card Values
walkthrough.text: Before we deal, let's understand card values. Face cards, meaning Kings, Queens, and Jacks, are each worth ten points. Aces are always worth one point. Number cards are worth their face value. So a seven is worth seven points and a three is worth three points.

walkthrough.title: Determine the Dealer
walkthrough.text: Each player draws a card from the deck. The player who draws the lower card becomes the dealer for the first hand. In case of a tie, draw again. The dealer alternates each hand after that.

walkthrough.title: Deal Ten Cards Each
walkthrough.text: The dealer shuffles the deck thoroughly and deals ten cards face down to each player, one at a time, alternating between players. Pick up your cards and look at them, but keep them hidden from your opponent.

walkthrough.title: Create the Stock and Discard Piles
walkthrough.text: Place the remaining 32 cards face down in the center of the table. This is the stock pile. Flip the top card of the stock pile face up and place it beside the stock. This card starts the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: First Card Option
walkthrough.text: The non-dealer gets the first option. They may take the face-up discard card or pass. If they pass, the dealer may take it or pass. If both pass, the non-dealer draws from the stock pile and the game begins normally.

walkthrough.title: Organize Your Hand
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to sort your hand. Look for potential melds, which are sets of three or four cards of the same rank, or runs of three or more consecutive cards in the same suit. Group these together so you can plan your strategy. Ready to play? Let's learn the rules.

bullets: Standard 52-card deck + pen and paper for scoring

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Gin Rummy is a two-player card game where you try to form your hand into melds while reducing your deadwood. Deadwood is any card in your hand that is not part of a meld. Your goal is to get your deadwood low enough to knock, or even better, to go Gin with zero deadwood.

walkthrough.title: What Are Melds
walkthrough.text: There are two types of melds. A set is three or four cards of the same rank, like three sevens or four Queens. A run is three or more consecutive cards of the same suit, like the four, five, and six of hearts. Each card can only belong to one meld.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Draw
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must first draw one card. You have two choices. Take the top card from the discard pile, which is face up so you know what you are getting. Or draw the top card from the stock pile, which is face down and a mystery. Pick whichever helps your hand more.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn: Discard
walkthrough.text: After drawing, you must discard one card face up onto the discard pile. You now have ten cards again. If you took the top card of the discard pile, you cannot discard that same card on this turn. You must discard a different card.

walkthrough.title: Deadwood Explained
walkthrough.text: Any card in your hand that is not part of a meld is called deadwood. The deadwood value is the sum of all your unmelded cards' point values. Face cards are worth ten, so holding even one unmelded King adds ten points of deadwood. Your goal is to reduce this number as quickly as possible.

walkthrough.title: Knocking
walkthrough.text: When your deadwood total is ten points or less, you may knock. To knock, discard your final card face down and lay your hand on the table. Separate your melds from your deadwood so both players can see them clearly. You do not have to knock when eligible. You can wait for a better opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Laying Off
walkthrough.text: After the knocker reveals their hand, the opponent gets a chance to lay off. Laying off means adding your unmelded cards to the knocker's melds. For example, if the knocker has three sevens and you have the fourth seven, you can lay it off to reduce your deadwood. Then both players compare remaining deadwood.

walkthrough.title: Going Gin
walkthrough.text: If you knock with zero deadwood, meaning every card in your hand is part of a meld, you have gone Gin. This is the best possible outcome. When you go Gin, your opponent cannot lay off any cards. You score your opponent's full deadwood value plus a 25-point bonus.

walkthrough.title: The Undercut
walkthrough.text: Here is a twist. If you knock and your opponent's deadwood, after laying off, is equal to or less than yours, your opponent undercuts you. The opponent scores the difference between the two deadwood totals plus a 25-point bonus. Getting undercut hurts, which is why knocking with very low deadwood is safer.

walkthrough.title: Stock Pile Runs Out
walkthrough.text: If only two cards remain in the stock pile and neither player has knocked, the hand is a draw. No points are scored and you redeal. This is called a stockout and it rarely happens in practice.

walkthrough.title: Hand Scoring
walkthrough.text: Let's summarize scoring for a single hand. If you knock normally, you score the difference between your opponent's deadwood and yours. If you go Gin, you score your opponent's deadwood plus 25 bonus points. If you get undercut, your opponent scores the deadwood difference plus 25 bonus points.

walkthrough.title: Game Scoring
walkthrough.text: Play continues across multiple hands until one player reaches 100 points. When that happens, the winner receives a 100-point game bonus. Each player also gets 25 bonus points for every hand they won, called box bonuses. If the loser never scored any points at all, it is a shutout, and all bonuses are doubled.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Gin Rummy
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first hand of Gin Rummy! Let's walk through a complete hand between two example players, Alice and Bob. Alice is the non-dealer. Follow along to see how drawing, discarding, and knocking work in practice.

walkthrough.title: The Opening Hands
walkthrough.text: Alice looks at her ten cards and spots a run: three, four, five of spades. She also has a pair of nines. Her deadwood includes a King, Queen, and Jack of different suits. That is 30 points of deadwood from face cards alone. Bob has two pairs and a run of two cards that needs a third.

walkthrough.title: Alice's First Turn
walkthrough.text: The face-up discard card is the six of spades. Alice takes it because it extends her run to three, four, five, six of spades. She discards her King of hearts, removing ten deadwood points. Great start.

walkthrough.title: Bob's First Turn
walkthrough.text: Bob sees Alice's discarded King of hearts. He does not need Kings, so he draws from the stock pile instead. He gets the nine of clubs, which gives him three nines. He discards a seven of diamonds that does not fit any meld.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: Alice notices Bob did not take the King. She now knows Bob probably does not need Kings or high hearts. She also notices Bob discarded a seven of diamonds. She can use this information later. Every discard tells a story about what your opponent is building.

walkthrough.title: Several Turns Later
walkthrough.text: After five turns, Alice has two melds and her deadwood is down to eight points. She could knock now since eight is under ten. But she has two cards that could form a third meld with one more draw. Should she knock or wait?

walkthrough.title: Alice Decides to Knock
walkthrough.text: Alice decides to knock with eight deadwood. She lays down her two melds and shows her deadwood cards. Bob reveals his hand. He has one completed meld of three nines. His deadwood totals 22 points. But Bob can lay off. He has a seven of spades that extends Alice's spade run. That removes seven deadwood from Bob's count, dropping him to 15.

walkthrough.title: Scoring the Hand
walkthrough.text: Alice's deadwood is eight. Bob's deadwood after laying off is 15. The difference is seven points in Alice's favor. Alice scores seven points for the hand. She writes it down. Nice play.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Holding Face Cards Too Long
walkthrough.text: In a different hand, Bob holds onto a King and Queen of clubs hoping to draw the Jack for a run. Three turns pass and the Jack never comes. Meanwhile, those two face cards are 20 points of deadwood. Alice knocks with low deadwood and wins big because Bob's hand is bloated with high-value cards.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Discard High Cards Early
walkthrough.text: The lesson here is simple. Face cards are worth ten points each, making them dangerous deadwood. If a face card is not part of a meld or very close to forming one, discard it early. Low cards like twos and threes are much safer to hold because they contribute minimal deadwood.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Waiting Too Long to Knock
walkthrough.text: Alice has nine deadwood and could knock. Instead, she waits, hoping for Gin. Three turns later, Bob knocks with five deadwood. Alice's hand has gotten worse because she drew cards that did not help. Now Bob scores and Alice's patience cost her the hand.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Knock Early When Able
walkthrough.text: Do not wait for Gin when you have low deadwood. A quick knock with a solid low count often beats the risk of waiting. Every turn you wait gives your opponent another chance to improve their hand and potentially knock first.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake: Ignoring the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: Bob has been drawing exclusively from the stock pile all game, ignoring the discard pile entirely. Meanwhile, Alice has been picking up discards that fit her melds perfectly. She completes three melds quickly while Bob is still searching randomly.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Watch the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: The discard pile is public information. When a card appears that fits your melds, take it. Also watch what your opponent takes and discards. If they pick up a six of hearts, they probably need hearts or sixes. Adjust your discards accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations on completing your first practice hand of Gin Rummy! You have seen how drawing, discarding, knocking, and laying off work. You have also learned three common mistakes: holding face cards too long, waiting too long to knock, and ignoring the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Before You Deal
walkthrough.text:
Before you play a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy on hand management and knock timing.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to melds, scoring, and card values.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the biggest mistakes new Gin Rummy players make. First, holding onto high-value face cards that are not forming melds. Second, waiting too long for Gin when a safe knock is available. Third, ignoring what your opponent picks up and discards. Fourth, not adapting when your planned melds are not coming together. Keep these in mind and you will win more hands.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Low Deadwood
walkthrough.text: Every card in your hand that is not in a meld counts against you. Prioritize discarding high-value deadwood first. A King in your hand is ten points of dead weight. Get rid of unproductive face cards quickly and hold onto low-value cards when possible. Keeping your deadwood low gives you the option to knock whenever you choose.

walkthrough.title: Triangle Cards Are Gold
walkthrough.text: The most valuable cards are what experienced players call triangle cards. These are cards that can contribute to both a set and a run. For example, the seven of hearts can pair with other sevens for a set, and it can also combine with the six and eight of hearts for a run. Triangle cards double your chances of completing a meld.

walkthrough.title: Knock Early
walkthrough.text: A quick knock with low deadwood is usually better than waiting for Gin. Gin scores 25 bonus points, which is great, but getting undercut costs you 25 bonus points. The risk often outweighs the reward. If your deadwood is four or less, consider knocking immediately. The undercut risk is very low at that point.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Discard Pile
walkthrough.text: The discard pile is a goldmine of information. Every card your opponent takes from the discard tells you something about their hand. If they grab the eight of diamonds, they likely need eights or a diamond run. Use this knowledge to avoid discarding cards they need. Defensive discarding is just as important as building your own melds.

walkthrough.title: Middle Cards Are More Flexible
walkthrough.text: Cards in the middle of the range, like fives, sixes, and sevens, have more meld potential than cards at the extremes. Aces can only form a run going up from ace-two-three. Kings can only form a run going down from King-Queen-Jack. But a six can go up or down in a run, making it more versatile. Prefer holding middle cards when choosing what to keep.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Discarding
walkthrough.text: When you know what your opponent is collecting, avoid giving them what they need. A safe discard is a card of the same rank as one your opponent recently discarded, because they clearly do not need that rank. Another safe option is a card adjacent to one they discarded, since they likely already abandoned that run.

walkthrough.title: Know When to Abandon a Plan
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the cards you need simply will not come. If you have been holding two cards waiting for a third to complete a meld for several turns, it might be time to cut your losses. Discard those speculative holds and focus on what you actually have. Flexibility beats stubbornness in Gin Rummy.

bullets: Don't hold high face cards that aren't forming melds
Don't wait too long for Gin when a safe knock is ready
Track what opponent picks up and discards
Adapt when planned melds aren't materializing

walkthrough.title: Early Game Hand Assessment
walkthrough.text: In the first two turns, assess your hand's potential. Count your existing melds and near-melds. If you have two melds already, plan to knock fast. If you have zero melds but several triangle cards, build patiently. Your opening assessment determines whether you play aggressively or conservatively.

walkthrough.title: Tracking the Deck
walkthrough.text: Advanced players keep a mental count of what has been played. Thirty-two cards start in the stock. Between discards and draws, cards cycle through. If you need the Jack of spades and you have seen zero Jacks discarded, all four are still live. If three Jacks have been discarded, your odds are very low. This mental math informs every draw decision.

walkthrough.title: The Knock Threshold
walkthrough.text: Experienced players rarely wait for Gin. The optimal knock threshold depends on how deep into the stock pile you are. In the first half of the hand, knock at six to eight deadwood to secure points before your opponent can organize. In the second half, if you still have not knocked, the risk of undercut rises. Knock at anything under ten to close out the hand.

walkthrough.title: Baiting Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: A subtle strategy is to discard cards that are adjacent to your melds, not in them. If you have a run of four-five-six of hearts, discard the three of hearts early. Your opponent may think hearts runs are cold and start discarding hearts to you. This is called salting the discard pile and it requires careful execution.

walkthrough.title: Undercut Defense
walkthrough.text: To minimize undercut risk, knock only when your deadwood is low enough that lay offs cannot save your opponent. If your deadwood is two and your opponent can lay off at most five points of deadwood, their remaining deadwood is still likely higher than yours. The lower your knock count, the safer you are.

walkthrough.title: Reading Opponent Draw Patterns
walkthrough.text: When your opponent draws from the stock pile multiple times in a row, they are not finding what they need in the discard. This suggests their hand is struggling. When they suddenly take from the discard pile, they have spotted a useful card. Track these patterns to estimate how close they are to knocking.

walkthrough.title: Game Score Awareness
walkthrough.text: Your strategy should shift based on the overall game score. If you are at 85 points and need only 15 more to win, play conservatively. Knock fast with any eligible deadwood to close out the game. If you are far behind, take more risks and aim for Gin to score the 25-point bonus that could swing the game.

walkthrough.title: The Shutout Strategy
walkthrough.text: If your opponent has scored zero points and you are winning, you are in shutout territory. A shutout doubles all end-of-game bonuses. This creates a powerful incentive to keep knocking fast and deny your opponent any points. Every hand you win without letting them score increases your final bonus dramatically.

2+ melds = plan to knock fast
0 melds + triangle cards = build patiently

entries.definition: A valid combination of cards in your hand. Either a set of three or four cards of the same rank, or a run of three or more consecutive cards of the same suit.
A meld of three or four cards of the same rank, such as three sevens or four Queens. Suits do not matter for sets.
A meld of three or more consecutive cards of the same suit, such as the four, five, and six of hearts. Aces are always low, so Ace-Two-Three is valid but Queen-King-Ace is not.
Cards in your hand that are not part of any meld. The total point value of your deadwood determines when you can knock and how scoring works.
Ending the hand by declaring your deadwood total is ten points or less. You lay down your melds, your opponent lays off, and deadwood totals are compared.
Knocking with zero deadwood, meaning all ten cards are in melds. Scores the opponent's full deadwood value plus a 25-point bonus. The opponent cannot lay off.

After the knocker reveals their melds, the opponent may add their unmelded cards to the knocker's melds. For example, adding a fourth card to a set of three. This reduces the opponent's deadwood count.
When the opponent's deadwood after laying off is equal to or less than the knocker's deadwood. The opponent scores the difference plus a 25-point bonus instead of the knocker scoring.
The face-down deck of remaining cards after dealing. Players draw from the stock pile when they do not want the discard pile card.
The face-up pile next to the stock pile. Players discard one card here each turn. The top card is available for the next player to take.
Kings, Queens, and Jacks. Each is worth ten points. High-value deadwood that should be discarded quickly if not part of a meld.
Always worth one point and always low. Aces can form the run Ace-Two-Three but cannot wrap around as Queen-King-Ace.
Cards numbered two through ten. Worth their face value in points. Middle-range cards (fives through sevens) have the most meld flexibility.

A card that can contribute to both a set and a run simultaneously. For example, the seven of hearts can pair with other sevens for a set, or with the six and eight of hearts for a run. These are the most valuable cards to hold.
Choosing which card to discard based on what your opponent needs. Safe discards include cards of the same rank as recently discarded cards, since the opponent clearly does not need that rank.
When only two cards remain in the stock pile and neither player has knocked. The hand is a draw with no points scored and a new hand is dealt.
The winner of the overall game, who first reaches 100 points, receives an additional 100-point bonus added to their final score.
Each player receives 25 bonus points for every individual hand they won during the game. Added to the final score after the game bonus.
Also called a schneider. Occurs when the losing player scored zero points across all hands. All end-of-game bonuses are doubled for the winner.
An optional rule where a player with eleven cards all in melds after drawing, without discarding, scores 31 bonus points instead of the standard 25. Not all groups play with this rule.
